notes_md:
**bánh mì** = Vietnamese sandwich (baguette with fillings). It can also mean *bread* in general, but most of the time people mean the sandwich.

It’s a countable noun. Use **cái** or **ổ** as the classifier:  

In the South, **ổ bánh mì** is very common for one sandwich; in the North, people often just say **cái bánh mì**.

To remember: *bánh* = cake/pastry type food, *mì* = wheat — so it literally hints at a wheat-based baked item.
exampleSentencesTranslated: This morning, I bought two Vietnamese sandwiches at the shop near my house.
She usually eats Vietnamese sandwich with eggs for breakfast.
I like to put a lot of vegetables in my Vietnamese sandwich.
notes:
bánh mì = Vietnamese sandwich (baguette with fillings). It can also mean bread in general, but most of the time people mean the sandwich.

It’s a countable noun. Use cái or ổ as the classifier:  

In the South, ổ bánh mì is very common for one sandwich; in the North, people often just say cái bánh mì.

To remember: bánh = cake/pastry type food, mì = wheat — so it literally hints at a wheat-based baked item.
exampleSentences: Sáng nay, anh mua hai cái bánh mì ở tiệm gần nhà.
Chị thường ăn bánh mì với trứng vào bữa sáng.
Em thích bỏ nhiều rau vào bánh mì của mình.
